preamble: The transformation from a voiced retroflex stop /ɖ/ to a voiceless aspirated retroflex affricate /ʈʂʰ/ involves a series of significant phonetic changes: devoicing, affrication, and aspiration. This complex shift represents a strengthening (fortition) of the consonant, moving from a simple voiced stop to a more complex voiceless aspirated affricate.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing (loss of vocal fold vibration), Affrication (release of a stop as a fricative, forming an affricate), Aspiration (addition of a puff of air following the release)

examples.note: This shift is inferred from documented historical changes in Chinese, where voiced stops were devoiced, retroflex stops merged into retroflex affricates, and stops became aspirated in certain tonal contexts.
